hook: You already know how to work with coordinates and vectors in two dimensions — now imagine peeling back the flat page and stepping into full three-dimensional space. The tools you built in Calc I and II only scratch the surface of what's possible when you add a third axis, a third component, and a whole new kind of multiplication. How do engineers describe the thrust of a rocket, or physicists model the spin of a charged particle in a magnetic field? The answer lives in this unit.
concept: Every topic here is about giving you precise algebraic and geometric language to describe position, direction, and shape in 3D space — unified by the idea that vectors are the fundamental objects that make higher-dimensional calculus possible, captured formally as elements of $\mathbb{R}^3$ equipped with the operations of the dot and cross products.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to locate any point in space using a three-axis coordinate system and calculate distances and midpoints with the 3D distance formula.
Master vector addition, scalar multiplication, and magnitude in $\mathbb{R}^3$, and use unit vectors to express any direction precisely.
Apply the dot product to calculate angles between vectors, test for perpendicularity, and decompose forces using scalar and vector projections.
Use the cross product to find a vector simultaneously perpendicular to two given vectors and to compute areas of parallelograms and volumes of parallelepipeds in 3D.
You will learn to write vector, parametric, and symmetric equations for lines, and derive normal-vector equations for planes, including finding intersections and distances between them.
Identify and sketch the six standard quadric surfaces — including ellipsoids, paraboloids, and hyperboloids — by analyzing their traces in coordinate planes.
